title: Sparkling bubbles in chiral active fluids
authors: Alessandro Petrini, Raphaël Maire, Umberto Marini Bettolo Marconi, Lorenzo Caprini
abstract: We study an inertial chiral active fluid, formed by repulsive particles that transfer angular momentum through odd interactions, i.e. transverse forces. Chirality induces an inhomogeneous phase, consisting of rotating bubbles, whose formation is favored at an optimal packing fraction. In this regime, we discover that bubbles may be dynamically unstable, breaking up and reforming in the steady state, thereby showing a spontaneous sparkling-like behavior reminiscent of supersaturated liquids. Bubbles and sparkling bubbles are predicted by a coarse-grained hydrodynamic theory, revealing the intrinsic non-linearity of these collective phenomena, and call for experimental verifications in granular spinners or spinning colloids.
summary: This paper identifies a new 'sparkling' phase in chiral active fluids where bubbles undergo continuous breakup and reformation. Driven by non-equilibrium transverse forces, this phase is explained through a hydrodynamic model showing that high chirality induces oscillations in bubble size. The findings provide a new way to understand complex, self-organizing patterns in active matter.
main_problem: The study investigates a novel, dynamically unstable phase in chiral active fluids called the 'sparkling bubble' (SBIO) phase, characterized by the continuous nucleation, deformation, and breakup of bubbles.
main_result: The authors discover that high chirality induces an underdamped oscillatory regime in bubble radii, leading to a 'sparkling' behavior where bubbles continuously merge and annihilate, distinct from the stable bubble (BIO) phase.
method: The researchers use 2D numerical Langevin simulations of repulsive particles and develop a coarse-grained hydrodynamic theory, including a generalized Rayleigh-Plesset approach to model bubble evolution.
model_or_system: A 2D inertial chiral active fluid composed of N repulsive particles interacting via a WCA potential and 'odd' transverse forces that break mirror symmetry.
key_observables: Vorticity, kinetic energy, bubble radius, normalized variance of bubble size, and the Minnaert frequency.
important_parameters: Chirality strength (omega/epsilon), packing fraction (phi), reduced temperature (Tr), and reduced inertial time (tau_I/tau).
main_assumptions: Neglect of spatial gradients in the velocity field for steady-state evaluation, assumption of perfectly circular bubbles, and neglect of viscous and odd-viscous contributions in the momentum equation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows snapshots of the three phases (homogeneous, BIO, SBIO), time-series of bubble breakup, and a U-shaped phase diagram. Figure 2 plots vorticity, kinetic energy, and bubble radius against chirality, using bubble size variance as an order parameter. Figure 3 shows vorticity and related dynamics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SBIO phase, describes the microscopic Langevin model and hydrodynamic derivation, presents simulation results and phase diagrams, and concludes with a theoretical explanation of the bubble instability mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Sparkling bubbles induced by odd interactions (SBIO). (a)–(c) Snapshot configurations of the homogeneous, bubble (BIO), and sparkling bubble (SBIO) phases, obtained at reduced chirality ω/ϵ = 600, 900, 1500 and packing fraction ϕ = 0.35. (d) Time evolution of a sparkling bubble (zoomed from (c)) at three different times for ω/ϵ = 1500 and ϕ = 0.35. (e) Phase diagram in the (ω/ϵ, ϕ) plane. In all panels, colors indicate the reference phase: homogeneous (gray), BIO (yellow), and SBIO (violet). The remaining simulation parameters are Tr = 20 and τI = m/γ = 10−2τ.
Figure 2. BIO and SBIO phases. (a) Average vorticity ⟨Ω⟩, (b) average kinetic energy ⟨K⟩, and (c) average bubble radius ⟨R⟩as functions of the chirality ω (rescaled by τ = σ p

Figure 3. Vorticity induced by sparkling behavior. (a) Vorticity distribution P(Ω) and (b) speed distribution P(|v|) for several values of the chirality ω (rescaled by ϵ), spanning the homogeneous, BIO, and SBIO phases. (c)-(f) Snapshots of the system showing the magnitude of the vorticity ((c) and (e)) and the magnitude of the transverse force ((d) and (f)) as a color gradient. Gray regions indicate areas devoid of particles. Panels (c) and (d) correspond to a BIO configuration (ω/ϵ = 900), while panels (e) and (f) correspond to a SBIO configuration (ω/ϵ = 1500). The remaining simulation parameters are ϕ = 0.35, Tr = 20, and τI = m/γ = 10−2τ.
